title: Sweeteners Guide
content:
GRANULATED SUGAR

Beet or cane; 99.5% sucrose. Standard for most cooking. One pound = approximately 2 cups.

Confectioners'/Powdered: 10X finest grind; contains cornstarch to prevent lumping. Measure as flour. Substitution: 1¾ cups confectioners' = 1 cup granulated. Heat over boiling water 10 minutes before using in uncooked icings to remove raw starch taste.

Superfine/Berry: Finer than granulated; best for meringues and sweetening fruits. Substitute cup for cup.

Brown Sugar: Moist beet or cane sugar with molasses (light or dark). Pack firmly when measuring; level with palm; unmold like sand castle. Substitution: 1 cup firmly packed brown = 1 cup granulated. Store tightly covered to prevent hardening. To soften: sprinkle with few drops water; heat in low oven briefly.

Raw/Turbinado: Partially refined, coarse, beige crystals. Substitute cup for cup for granulated; heavier molasses flavor.

Maple Sugar: Strong, sweet taste. Grate or sliver before using. Substitution: ⅓ cup maple sugar = 1 cup granulated.

LIQUID SWEETENERS

Corn Syrup: Dextrose and glucose. Light (standard) and dark (stronger). Substitute 2 cups corn syrup for 1 cup sugar; never replace more than half sugar in recipe; reduce other liquid ¼ cup per 2 cups syrup substituted.

Honey: Levulose and dextrose; high moisture. Pasteurization reduces nutrient value. Substitution: 1 cup honey for 1⅓ cups sugar; reduce liquid ¼ cup; add pinch of soda to neutralize acidity unless sour milk is in recipe. Higher heat needed for candies and jellies. Children under 1 year: do not use (botulism risk).

Molasses: Rich in iron; improves keeping quality. Three types:
- Unsulfured: Best quality from sun-ripened cane
- Sulfured: By-product of sugar making; sulfur retained
- Blackstrap: Third boiling residue; high minerals but unpalatable

Substitution: 1 cup molasses for ¾ cup granulated sugar; replace no more than half sugar in recipe; add ½ teaspoon soda per cup molasses; reduce other liquid 5 tablespoons per cup molasses.

Maple Syrup: Minimum 11 lbs per gallon if labeled "pure." Store covered at room temperature; refrigerate after opening. If mold develops, strain and bring to rolling boil before rebottling. Substitution: ¾ cup maple syrup per 1 cup sugar; reduce liquid 3 tablespoons per cup syrup.

Caramelized Sugar: For hard glaze, heat 1 cup sugar in heavy non-ferrous pan over low heat, stirring constantly 8-10 minutes until straw-colored. Remove from heat; add ¼ cup very hot water slowly (careful of spurting). Return to low heat 8-10 minutes until maple syrup color. Store covered; hardens on standing—reheat jar in hot water to liquefy.
